coredata.dc:title: New insight into multi-functional MOG@COP/SA beads for co-adsorption and mono-detection in CTC-Cr(Ⅵ) co-contamination system and efficient anti-counterfeiting

coredata.dc:description: Co-contamination of antibiotics and heavy metals in water environment has provoked considerable toxicological concerns. Meantime, residue detection of heavy metals as well as national anti-counterfeiting safety also have driven attempts in wastewater treatment. In this paper, a core–shell hybrid MOG@COP is designed with behaviors superior to pristine components by combining a Zr-based metal–organic gel and a guanidine-based covalent organic polymer, for co-adsorption and mono-detection in chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTC)-Cr(Ⅵ) co-contamination system and efficient anti-counterfeiting. In single/binary system, the adsorption capacities of CTC and Cr(Ⅵ) behave as 133.69 and 63.45 mg·g−1/181.82 and 64.47 mg·g−1, and the high sensitivity and low detection limit of Cr(Ⅵ) render as 1.46*105 M−1 and 0.20 μM/1.49*102 M−1 and 0.63 mM, respectively. In addition, to solve the powder shortcomings of easy agglomeration and difficult recycling, MOG@COP is fixed into sodium alginate (SA) matrix to fabricate MOG@COP/SA beads, yielding reinforced adsorption performance of CTC/Cr(Ⅵ) in both single system (152.91/92.25 mg·g−1) and binary system (174.83/75.30 mg·g−1). More importantly, a self-constructed adsorption-detection-anti-counterfeiting (ADA) platform is ingeniously developed with the MOG@COP/SA beads as the backfills for continuously processing CTC-Cr(Ⅵ) co-contaminated water, realizing real-time visualization of Cr(Ⅵ) adsorption process and optical anti-counterfeiting protection. In conclusion, this work is expected to not only trigger the reasonable design of the core–shell hybrid MOG@COP/SA beads with versatile performances of simultaneous luminescence monitor, capture and anti-counterfeiting, but also allow the original exploration for all-in-one platform.

head.ce:title: New insight into multi-functional MOG@COP/SA beads for co-adsorption and mono-detection in CTC-Cr(Ⅵ) co-contamination system and efficient anti-counterfeiting

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Co-contamination of antibiotics and heavy metals in water environment has provoked considerable toxicological concerns. Meantime, residue detection of heavy metals as well as national anti-counterfeiting safety also have driven attempts in wastewater treatment. In this paper, a core–shell hybrid MOG@COP is designed with behaviors superior to pristine components by combining a Zr-based metal–organic gel and a guanidine-based covalent organic polymer, for co-adsorption and mono-detection in chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTC)-Cr(Ⅵ) co-contamination system and efficient anti-counterfeiting. In single/binary system, the adsorption capacities of CTC and Cr(Ⅵ) behave as 133.69 and 63.45 mg·g−1/181.82 and 64.47 mg·g−1, and the high sensitivity and low detection limit of Cr(Ⅵ) render as 1.46*105 M−1 and 0.20 μM/1.49*102 M−1 and 0.63 mM, respectively. In addition, to solve the powder shortcomings of easy agglomeration and difficult recycling, MOG@COP is fixed into sodium alginate (SA) matrix to fabricate MOG@COP/SA beads, yielding reinforced adsorption performance of CTC/Cr(Ⅵ) in both single system (152.91/92.25 mg·g−1) and binary system (174.83/75.30 mg·g−1). More importantly, a self-constructed adsorption-detection-anti-counterfeiting (ADA) platform is ingeniously developed with the MOG@COP/SA beads as the backfills for continuously processing CTC-Cr(Ⅵ) co-contaminated water, realizing real-time visualization of Cr(Ⅵ) adsorption process and optical anti-counterfeiting protection. In conclusion, this work is expected to not only trigger the reasonable design of the core–shell hybrid MOG@COP/SA beads with versatile performances of simultaneous luminescence monitor, capture and anti-counterfeiting, but also allow the original exploration for all-in-one platform.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The co-contamination phenomenon of typical antibiotics (e.g., CTC) and heavy metals (e.g., Cr(VI)) in water environment has become increasingly serious, by reason of the developed livestock/poultry industry and agricultural fertilizer/pesticide industry [1,2]. If inappropriately tackled, the chemical, physical as well as biological characters of water might be altered by the combined pollution. Both of antibiotics and heavy metals have great harm to human health and ecological environment, especially the co-presence with boosted negative impact compared to the individual contaminants, owing to their different fates and transport mechanisms [3,4]. Therefore, it is imperative to take effective measures to handle with water co-contaminated with CTC and Cr(VI) and investigate thoroughly the co-removal mechanisms [5,6]. Remarkably, adsorption has been acknowledged as a popular purification technology for eliminating both inorganic and organic pollutants from aquatic environment, by virtue of the operation simplicity, high-performance efficiency, economy affordability and scope adaptability [7]. One limitation of the developed adsorbents involving activated carbon [8], zeolite [9], mesoporous silicon [10], metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) [11] and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [12] however is that they center on unitary function of removing CTC or Cr(VI). Recently, the Liang’s and Yan’s groups studied the removal of Cr(VI) and CTC from water by a new type of peel based biochar composite (FPBC) and N-doped porous carbon (NC), respectively [13,14]. Based on the above content, how to manufacture novel adsorbents to co-remove CTC and Cr(VI) is undoubtedly a heavy-weight challenge.

The excessive content of Cr(VI) in the drinking water caused by the increasing release into the environment deserves special mention. To protect people from Cr(VI), the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and World Health Organization (WHO) have set the maximum limits for Cr(VI) concentration in drinking water as 0.10 mg·L−1 and 0.05 mg·L−1, respectively [15]. Accordingly, it is worth pointing out that the detection of Cr(VI) is of a vital importance in medical, ecological and environmental aspects. The commonly used methods for detecting Cr(VI) include liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [16], ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometry [17], capillary electrophoresis (CE) etc. [18,19]. Nevertheless, the necessary sample preparation process is time-consuming, operation-complicated and equipment-expensive [19]. In comparison, smartphone-based fluorescence visual detection method relies on the advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity and convenient operation, and what’s more, have received a great deal of concentration as a portable field platform for sensing applications. Specifically, the smartphone-based fluorescence sensing system is composed of an ultraviolet (UV) light excitation light source, a smartphone detector and a third-party app software to translate the fluorescence intensity from the RGB (red, green, blue) component values of the images [20]. Although great achievements have been made for visual detection of heavy metals, there is still plenty room for exploring improvement, such as establishing a relationship with Cr(VI) has not been widespread [21]. Also, as far as we know, there are few reports about the simultaneous capture and detection of Cr(VI), in particular, in the co-contaminated system.

Metal-organic gels (MOGs), which are constituted by mismatched self-assembly of organic ligands and metal ions, holding unreacted functional groups, inherent open metal sites, tunable pore architectures and high surface area [22], have come to our vision in catalysis [23], sensing [24], drug delivery [25] and adsorption [26]. Notably, water-stable MOGs spiked the interest in solving with antibiotics in wastewater treatment [27]. Likewise, covalent organic polymers (COPs), one kind of rapidly rising races typically constructed by one or two organic building blocks with multiple active ends through covalent self-condensation, also accept intense research interest in molecular separation and catalysis, particularly in wastewater treatment [28–30]. As awareness of the fact, multiple ligands or multi-component synthetic strategy has been adopted into MOGs or COPs synthesis to touch far more opportunities in the various structures, versatile functionalities and notable properties to overcome the dependence on the specific bonding instead of multiple irregular bonding [31–34]. Recently, it is proposed to integrate MOFs and COFs to form hybrid MOF@COF or MOF/COF (usually connected by covalent bonds), could not only retain the structural traits and inherit characteristic functions of individual components, but also generate new integrated properties, which is ideal for constructing multi-functional platforms. Besides, the practical applications of powdered porous materials are restricted by some burdened disadvantages, such as difficulty in recycling, problem in carrying, easy agglomeration and potential secondary pollution [35]. To address the aforementioned issues, as reported, integrating powdered porous materials onto a certain carrier is an effective approach. Focus on the natural polymers, for instance, chitosan, cellulose and sodium alginate, are commonly employed due to their inherent hydrophilicity, plasticity, recyclability, biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, and cost-effectiveness [36].

In the above sense, we report a strategy for organically integration of water-stable carboxyl-functionalized Zr-MOGs and fluorescent ionic three-component amide-functionalized COPs through hydrogen bondings to construct a multi-functional hierarchical hybrid material, i.e., core–shell MOG@COP, for the co-adsorption and mono-detection in Cr(VI)-CTC co-contamination system. On the one hand, we select 4,4′,4′′-triazine-2,4,6-triyl-tribenzoate (TATB) as the organic ligand, and the Zr4+ as the metal center to synthesize MOGs with great potential in removing CTC from water. On the other hand, fluorescent and positively charged COPs with collaborative adsorption and detection for anion Cr(VI) were recruited by employing three monomers of benzene-1,3,5-tricarbohydrazide (BTCH), 1,3-diaminoguanidine hydrochloride (DgCl), and 2,2′-bipyridine-5,5′-dicarbaldehyde (Bp) reasonably. To be precise, wherein the three come from tridentate aromatic amine with hydrogen bonding sites, positive delocalized triangular conjugated guanidine with anion binding ability and N-rich fluorescent conjugated system with strong N atom chelation [37]. As a proof-of-concept application, the obtained fluorescent MOG@COP is anticipated to be used as an effective multi-functional platform in CTC-Cr(VI) co-contamination system for co-adsorption of CTC and Cr(VI) and mono-detection of Cr(VI). Synchronously, sodium alginate (SA) is adopted to work as the carrier to gain easily recycled MOG@COP/SA beads by encapsulating the powder MOG@COP. Furthermore, the adsorption and co-adsorption behaviors for CTC and Cr(VI) of MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads in both single and binary systems were studied, and proposed the main adsorption mechanism. Moreover, the detection behavior and sensing mechanism were discussed based on the quenching effect of Cr(VI) on MOG@COP. Aside from that, it was determined that MOG@COP/SA beads can provide real-time visual detection of Cr(VI) concentration in water samples through a smartphone analysis application. Additionally, the potential of fluorescent MOG@COP/SA beads in anti-counterfeiting technology was demonstrated. More importantly, to broaden the accessibility of field applications, MOG@COP/SA beads were implemented into a self-constructed ADA platform to evaluate the long-term performance for the co-removal of CTC and Cr(VI), on-site monitor of Cr(VI) and optical anti-counterfeiting. This work opens up a new avenue for the reasonable design of the versatile core–shell hybrid MOG@COP/SA beads, furnishes a feasible exploration for naked-eye detection of Cr(Ⅵ) and co-remediation of CTC-Cr(Ⅵ) co-polluted wastewater, and above all, provides worthwhile insights in the prevention of counterfeiting and shoddy (Scheme 1 ).
The details of materials and characterization of this work were reflected in Supporting information.

TATB (0.36 mmol, 0.1576 g) and ZrOCl2·8H2O (0.54 mmol, 0.1726 g) were mixed in DMSO (5.00 mL) and C2H5OH (1.00 mL) in a glass vessel. The above mixed solutions were transferred into an electric drying chamber and maintained at 120 °C for 12 h. Furthermore, the obtained wet gels were put into dialysis bags in distilled water and then vacuum dried in a lyophilizer to yield the white Zr-MOG powder.

The monomers of BTCH (0.25 mmol, 0.0630 g), DgCl (0.30 mmol, 0.0377 g) and Bp (0.60 mmol, 0.1273 g) were added in a glass bottle and dissolved in DMSO (5.00 mL). The mixture above was then heated at 100 ℃ for 0.5 min. After that, the obtained wet gels were put into dialysis bags in distilled water and then vacuum dried in a lyophilizer to yield the orange COP powder.

The prepared Zr-MOG powder (0.0477 g) was added to the above-mentioned synthesis procedure of COP without any adjustment. Finally, the orange-yellow MOG@COP powder was obtained through the similar freeze-drying procedure.

SA (2.00 % w/v) was dissolved in distilled water under magnetic stirring, followed by the addition of MOG@COP (2.00 % w/v). Afterwards, the above well-dispersed mixture was transferred into the syringe, and dropwise into CaCl2 solution (1.00 % w/v) to acquire beads. The obtained composite beads (namely MOG@COP/SA beads) were filtered and rinsed 3–4 times with distilled water to remove the surplus Ca2+ from their surface. The pure SA beads were fabricated by a similar method as mentioned above. The difference was the absence of MOG@COP.

The prepared Zr-MOG, COP, MOG@COP, pure SA beads and MOG@COP/SA beads were put through the freeze-drying procedure to obtain dried samples. The former SEM images revealed that Zr-MOG was composed of stacked nanoparticles and COP presented dry and flat fibrous shape (Fig. 1 a, b), yet latter SEM and TEM images clearly stated that MOG@COP with MOG as core and COP as shell (Fig. 1c, d). Moreover, according to the SEM-EDS mapping, the obvious signals of C, N, O and Zr could be observed on the whole section of MOG@COP, proving that it was uniform without aggregation phenomenon (Fig. 1e, f and Fig. S1). The referred above verified that the success in synthesizing composite MOG@COP material. As illustrated in Fig. 1g, h, the surface of pure SA beads exhibited smoothly, while MOG@COP/SA beads uncovered rough surfaces due to the introduction of MOG@COP. Concurrently, the digital photos of as-prepared pure white SA beads and orange-yellow MOG@COP/SA beads were shown in Fig. 1i, attesting that MOG@COP was embedded in SA flawlessly.

The amorphous characteristics of Zr-MOG and COP were confirmed by XRD analysis. Apparently, the MOG@COP revealed the low intensity dispersion peaks of Zr-MOG and COP, manifesting that the composite between Zr-MOG and COP was realized. Thereafter, it was noticeable that the pattern of MOG@COP/SA beads was in accordance with MOG@COP, whilst the pattern of SA beads did not display conspicuous peaks, testifying that the structure of MOG@COP was primly preserved after shaping (Fig. S2a, b). Otherwise, the chemical structure of the synthetic materials was determined by FT-IR analysis (Fig. 1j). Superior results of Zr-MOG were seen that the peaks at 489 and 769 cm−1 were derived from the stretching vibrations of the ZrO and ZrOZr bonds [38,39], while the peaks at 1701, 1359 and 1519 cm−1 were derived from free −COOH and relevant to the deprotonated −COO− of the synthesized material, respectively, confirming that Zr4+ was successfully loaded on the TATB ligand [40]. Significantly, the CN bonds of COP at 1176 and 1359 cm−1 were stemmed from DgCl and Bp, respectively. At the same time, the COP displayed the disappeared primary sharp amine peaks of the absorption peaks of DgCl and BTCH at 3251, 3313 and 3294 cm−1 [41], implying that the amino groups of monomer BTCH and DgCl was consumed in the reaction process, wherein, hydrazone linkages were formed between the aldehyde of Bp and the hydrazide of BTCH (Fig. S3) [37]. For MOG@COP, the new peak at 1355 cm−1 generated from the formation of hydrogen bondings between −COOH and −NHCO. Subsequently, for pure SA beads, peaks at 1030 and 3380 cm−1 represented the stretching vibrations of COC and −OH, respectively. In particular, the peak at 3423 cm−1 (−OH) in MOG@COP shifted to 3421 cm−1 in MOG@COP/SA beads, thus confirming the existence of hydrogen bondings between MOG@COP and SA [27].

Further XPS analysis was performed to evaluate the elemental composition and chemical state of the obtained materials (Fig. 1k and Fig. S4). Specifically, for Zr-MOG, the four peaks were 284.53 eV, 285.28 eV, 286.90 eV and 288.91 eV involved the CC/CC, CN/CN, CO and OCO bonds in the C 1 s spectrum; the peak with binding energy of 398.77 eV was designated as N 1 s in the organic ligand (Fig. S4a, b) [42]. The binding energies of 530.18 eV, 531.75 eV and 533.34 eV, respectively, corresponding to the ZrO, CO and CO bonds in the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S4c) [43]. Not only that, two peaks of Zr 3d at 182.75 and 185.12 eV were ascribed to Zr 3d5/2 and Zr 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. S4d). Then, for COP, the three peaks of 284.58 eV (CO), 285.30 eV (CN) and 288.03 eV (CC/CC), the peaks at binding energies of 398.69 eV (C=N), 400.01 eV (CN) and 531.58 eV (CO) were designated as C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s spectra (Fig. S4a, b, c) [37,41]. Thirdly, in the survey spectrum of MOG@COP, there were four elements: C, N, O and Zr, manifesting the triumphantly combined Zr-MOG with COP (Fig. 1k). Ulteriorly, both binding energies of −OH and −NH were changed when compared with the peaks of O 1 s of Zr-MOG and N 1 s of COP, respectively, which proved once again that Zr-MOG and COP may be connected by hydrogen bondings (Fig. S4b, c). Aside from this, peaks at 347.42 eV and 351.07 eV were identified as Ca 2p3/2 and Ca 2p1/2 of SA beads (Fig. S4h). Eventually, for MOG@COP/SA beads, the newly generated characteristic peaks of Ca 2p convincingly verified the incorporation of SA [27].

The pore size distribution and BET and Langmuir specific surface areas of Zr-MOG, COP, MOG@COP, MOG@COP/SA beads and SA beads were gauged by N2 adsorption–desorption experiments (Fig. 1l, m and Fig. S5). We described the results of the BET and Langmuir surface areas of Zr-MOG (COP, MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads) as 327.51 (24.91, 73.19 and 13.66) and 499.714 (44.70, 121.80 and 21.13) m2·g−1. The reduced BET and Langmuir surface areas of MOG@COP/SA beads compared to MOG@COP can be deduced from the involvement of SA with low surface areas of 11.20 m2·g−1 (S BET) and 19.75 m2·g−1 (S Langmuir). As well as, TGA results were performed to evaluate the thermal stability of the Zr-MOG, COP, MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads (Fig. S6). The results demonstrated that the temperature order of the structure collapse was Zr-MOG (487.99 ℃) >MOG@COP (332.88 ℃) >COP (332.79 ℃) >MOG@COP/SA beads (229.52 ℃), exhibiting their superior thermal stability. Furthermore, we have provided the SEM, FT-IR and XRD images of Zr-MOG before and after CTC adsorption (CTC@Zr-MOG) in Fig. S7. It is remarkable that the morphologies of adsorbents have not changed after adsorption. Meanwhile, the structure of adsorbents before and after CTC adsorption were compared, suggesting that their structures had no changes. Based on them, the good stability of adsorbents has been demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption kinetics and isotherms of Zr-MOG and COP
ce:section.ce:para: So as to reveal the adsorption behavior of CTC by Zr-MOG and Cr(Ⅵ) by COP, the adsorption kinetics were studied as priority and evaluated by pseudo-first-order (PFO) kinetic model, pseudo-second-order (PSO) kinetic model along with intra-particle diffusion (IPD) model (Fig. S8, Table S1). Clearly, compared with PFO kinetics, PSO kinetics matched well in describing kinetic adsorption data counting on the higher correlation coefficients and lesser mean relative error values, which was consistent with chemisorption as a rate-dominant mechanism. While in the case of fitting with the IPD model, the adsorption processes of CTC by Zr-MOG and Cr(Ⅵ) by COP can both be divided into three stages, expressing that multiple rate control steps may be involved (Tables S2–S5). Furthermore, Freundlich and Langmuir models were used to simulate the adsorption isotherms of CTC by Zr-MOG and Cr(Ⅵ) by COP to study adsorption mechanism and calculate the adsorption capacity (Fig. S9, Table S10). Accordingly, there is no doubt that the Langmuir model was more reasonable than the Freundlich model, indicating that removal of either CTC or Cr(VI) was more likely to occur through a single-layer mode, proving that the Zr-MOG or COP surface was uniformly covered by pollutants (Table S11) [40]. Remarkably, the Langmuir maximum adsorption capacities (q m) of CTC by Zr-MOG and Cr(Ⅵ) by COP were 166.94 mg·g−1 and 63.33 mg·g−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption performances of MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of highlighting the influence of various factors on the composite materials, MOG@COP was selected to run the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of pH of MOG@COP
ce:section.ce:para: The pH values of the solutions were closely associated with target contaminants in water [44]. Therefore, the effect of initial pH upon CTC and Cr(VI) elimination from water by MOG@COP was experimentally investigated, where the impact in the surface charge of the adsorbent and the existence form of contaminants may take certain responsibility. Consequently, the point of zero charge (pHzpc) of MOG@COP obtained by zeta potential analysis was approximately 8.46. A series of adsorption experiments were established to calculate the adsorption amounts (q e) of the MOG@COP at pH values ranging from 2.00 to 10.00. For CTC molecule, when pH<3.30, it mainly occurred in the form of cation (CTCH3+); when 3.30 < pH<7.44, zwitterionic CTC (CTCH2±) had a dominant position with the decrease of cationic CTC; when pH>7.44, CTC as anion species resided in solution (CTCH−, 7.44 < pH<9.27; CTC2−, pH>9.27) (Fig. 2 a) [45]. In the light of the influence of pH, it can be drawn that the q e of MOG@COP raised when pH value increased from 2.00 to 8.00, whereas it slightly reduced when pH turned to 10.00 (Fig. 2b). At pH=2.00, since MOG@COP and CTC both provided positive surface charges, the existence of electrostatic repulsions could hinder the adsorption of CTC. Raising pH values from 4.00 to 8.00, the surface charge properties of MOG@COP remained invariant but CTC molecules changed from positively charged to negatively charged, therefore engendering electrostatic attraction to achieve optimal CTC adsorption efficiency. When the pH value exceeded 9.27, electrostatic repulsion was responsible for a slight decrease in the adsorption capacity. The aforesaid content proclaimed that the adsorption performance of MOG on CTC was stable in weak acid, neutral and alkaline conditions. For Cr(VI), taking pH=6.50 as the boundary value, its main forms were HCrO4 − and CrO4 2−, respectively (Fig. 2c) [46]. As can be seen from Fig. 2b, when pH<8.00, positive MOG@COP with negative HCrO4 − and CrO4 2− promoted the adsorption interaction through electrostatic attraction. On the contrary, at pH=10.00, the negative MOG@COP produced electrostatic repulsion with CrO4 2−, giving rise to a drop in the q e of MOG@COP for Cr(VI). Moreover, OH− and CrO4 2− anions could compete for adsorption sites, inducing the removal efficiency of Cr(VI) was reduced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of dosage of MOG@COP
ce:section.ce:para: The dosage made a difference on the removal efficiencies of CTC and Cr(VI) and the adsorption capacity of MOG@COP. As represented in Fig. 2d, with the dosage of MOG@COP expanded from 0.25 g·L−1 to 2.00 g·L−1, on account of the substantial increase of the contact surface area, exposure of plenty of adsorption active sites and the reduction of contaminant exposure per unit weight of the adsorbent, the removal rates of CTC and Cr(VI) were enhanced while the adsorption capacity of MOG@COP decreased. After considering the above two aspects, 1.00 g·L−1 was concluded as the optimal dosage of adsorbent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of coexisting ionic species and strength of MOG@COP
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the existence of salt in actual water, the effects of inorganic ionic species and strength on the adsorption of CTC and Cr(VI) by MOG@COP were studied. It can be seen from Fig. 2e that the influence of coexisting inorganic ions on the adsorption of CTC and Cr(VI) by MOG@COP exerted a negligible effect. The effect of HCO3 − on the adsorption of Cr(VI) by MOG@COP, however, was an exception. What failed to appreciate was that the pH value enlarged by the hydrolysis of HCO3 − made it difficult to eliminate Cr(VI) under alkaline conditions, which was tally with the discussion results about the pH effect. Beyond that, owing to the structural similarity of HCO3 − and HCrO4 −, they may compete for certain specific adsorption sites, hence once more making for reduced adsorption of Cr(VI) [47]. In general, ionic strength could alter pore filling effect, electrostatic interaction and hydrophobic interaction between adsorbents and contaminants [48,49]. However, the amount of CTC captured by MOG@COP generated a negligible difference with the change of NaCl concentration, which further confirmed its outstanding anti-interference performance (Fig. 2f) [50]. Associating with the ascending NaCl concentrations, the adsorption of Cr(VI) by MOG@COP was inhibited by 24.82 % at 0.20 mol·L−1, and then tended towards equilibrium. Together, the present findings confirmed MOG@COP owned excellent anti-interference capability and application prospect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of humic acid of MOG@COP
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the fact that humic acid (HA) presents extensively in water environment, it is extremely crucial to study its influence on CTC/Cr(VI) adsorptive removal process. As exhibited in Fig. 2g, the removal rate of CTC boosted with the rise of HA concentration. It can be explained in two aspects: one is that when the HA concentration was low, HA occupied the adsorption sites through electrostatic attraction, inhibiting the removal of CTC, another is that the raising free HA molecules were adsorbed on the surface of the MOG@COP through hydrogen bondings and π–π interaction, thus promoting the removal of CTC. Summarize as that the latter gets prominent because it can overcome electrostatic attraction [27]. The effect of HA on Cr(VI) can also be categorized into two-fold: one side is that electronegative HA competed with Cr(VI) ions in solution for the positive charge sites on the surface of MOG@COP, thus blocking the surface and inhibiting the removal; another side is that HA constricted the adsorption of polyvalent metal contaminants on the adsorbent by forming complexes with the target metal oxyanions, which enhanced the electrostatic repulsion between Cr(VI) and the surface of MOG@COP, thereby obstructing their interaction [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of contact time/concentration and recyclability of MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads
ce:section.ce:para: As expressed in Fig. 3 a, c and Tables S2–S5, with the change of contact time and concentration, obviously, that the PSD kinetics model and Freundlich model possessed a good performance in describing the adsorption of CTC/Cr(Ⅵ) by both MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads. The adsorption sites on the surface of MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads, in turn, were heterogeneous and multilayered, which may be originated from the diverse and multiplicate adsorption sites of two components of Zr-MOG and COP in MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads (Figs. S10, S11) [52]. As expected, the MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads inherited the functionalities of primitive counterparts in the hold of q m of CTC (133.69 mg·g−1 and 152.91 mg·g−1) and Cr(Ⅵ) (63.45 mg·g−1 and 92.25 mg·g−1) by Zr-MOG and COP, respectively (Fig. 3b, d, Fig. S12 and Table S11). From the short review above, key findings emerge: (i) the removal ability of bifunctional hybrid materials (MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads) for either CTC or Cr(VI) was at the top of reported single functional adsorbent for CTC/Cr(VI) (Fig. 3e, Table S14); (ii) the hybrid materials shrank a light for CTC removal efficiency, whereas the Cr(VI) maintained unchanged; (iii) despite the poor adsorption ability of pure SA beads, MOG@COP/SA beads exhibited enhanced adsorption ability in contrast with the MOG@COP. This appeared to be a case of the interaction between various electron-rich groups in CTC molecules (such as hydroxyl, carbonyl and amino groups) and Ca2+ in MOG@COP/SA beads through n-π electron donor acceptor interaction (n-π EDA), enabling strong deposition of CTC molecules on the surface of the adsorbent [53].

Since the reusable performance of adsorbents was an important parameter to measure their practical application feasibleness, it was necessary to evaluate adsorption–desorption recyclability (Fig. 2h). Specifically, the CTC/Cr(Ⅵ)-loaded adsorbents were regenerated by adopting methanol/0.10 % hydrochloric acid solution as eluent at room temperature. After five adsorption–desorption cycles of MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads, the removal rates of CTC/Cr(Ⅵ) were still 50.00 %/91.66 % and 57.62 %/81.11 %, respectively. Surprisingly, the reusability of MOG@COP/SA beads was improved compared to MOG@COP, declaring that MOG@COP/SA beads with shaping appearance and morphology offset the defect of hard reclaiming for powder samples.

The co-adsorption kinetics and isotherms of CTC and Cr(Ⅵ) by MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads in CTC-Cr(Ⅵ) binary system were comprehended with a similar outcome of PSD model and Freundlich model matched. We compared the key parameters in binary system to single system and, aimed to explore deep insight to the synergistic and competitive mechanisms. To begin with the adsorption rate, both the correspondence of CTC by MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads were significantly strengthened (Figs. S13–S15 and Tables S6–S9). For the same q e, MOG@COP (MOG@COP/SA beads) required 12 (60) h in single system, but only 1.00 (0.50) h in binary system. In subsequence, in regard to the q m, the CTC counter values (181.82 mg·g−1 for MOG@COP and 174.83 mg·g−1 for MOG@COP/SA beads) were upgraded, which can be explained by the synergistic effect rather than the competitive effect in the co-existence of CTC and Cr(Ⅵ). Nevertheless, the adsorption rates of Cr(VI) in binary and single system were basically identical, only the Cr(VI) counter values (64.47 mg·g−1 for MOG@COP and 75.30 mg·g−1 for MOG@COP/SA beads) were slightly suppressed (Fig. S16 and Table S12). The specific causes were elaborated in the adsorption mechanism. Impressively, the ease-of-reusability was obvious reflected in binary system, whereby the eluent was changed into acidic methanol of 0.10 % hydrochloric acid. With respect to five-time used MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads, the CTC/Cr(Ⅵ) removal rates came to 51.10 %/100.00 % and 71.10 %/94.95 %, respectively, that go beyond the single system (Fig. 2h). Incredibly, the average costs of MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads to remove CTC and Cr(Ⅵ) from wastewater was estimated to be approximately 0.0696 and 0.0399 RMB per gram (Table S13), which were feasible for large-scale production of industrial-grade materials and greatly expanded the application of composite materials in various fields.

Herein, we take MOG@COP to exemplify the adsorption mechanism as displayed in Fig. 5a. The BET results of MOG@COP after adsorption (CTC@MOG@COP, Cr(Ⅵ)@MOG@COP and CTC-Cr(Ⅵ)@MOG@COP reduced to 52.60, 62.87 and 63.03 m2·g−1 (Fig. S17)) testified that the large surface area and porous structure provided easy diffusion channels and abundant active adsorption sites to impel CTC/Cr(Ⅵ) propitiously migrated from liquid phase to the active sites of solid porous MOG@COP, which were dominated by the pore filling effect (PF). Besides, we can explore the effect of initial solution pH and co-existing ions on the electrostatic interaction (ES) between MOG@COP and CTC/Cr(Ⅵ), which should be a critical mechanism to drive the adsorption of CTC/Cr(Ⅵ). According to the FT-IR and XPS spectra of CTC@MOG@COP, Cr(Ⅵ)@MOG@COP and CTC-Cr(Ⅵ)@MOG@COP, hydrogen bonding interaction (HB) generally occurred between contaminants and adsorbents. Specifically, compared with MOG@COP, a wider peak of the − OH/−C=O/−NH group appeared at 3350 − 3450/1490 − 1540/2850 − 2920 cm−1 after adsorption, expressing that hydrogen bondings were formed between MOG@COP and CTC/Cr(Ⅵ) (Fig. S18a) [54]. Otherwise, the binding energy of C=O/C−O migrated from 531.92/533.42 eV to 531.84/533.38 eV, 531.82/533.40 eV and 531.80/533.30 eV after adsorbing CTC, Cr(Ⅵ) and CTC-Cr(Ⅵ), manifesting that HB may pertain (Fig. S19) [40].

Meanwhile, the hydrophilic/hydrophobic groups (triazines, carboxyl and hydroxyl/aromatic rings) contained in MOG@COP promoted further adsorption of amphiphilic CTC molecules [55]. The hydrophilic-hydrophobic effect (HH) may form a balance in the adsorption process. Apart from that, the characteristic peaks, representing the C−C/C=C and O−C=O, moved from 284.53 (288.91) eV in the MOG@COP to 284.60 (288.50) eV and 288.58 (288.47) eV in the CTC@MOG@COP and CTC-Cr(Ⅵ)@MOG@COP, respectively, verifying the occurrence of π–π electron-donor–acceptor interaction (π–π EDA) (Fig. S18b) [56]. Moreover, the binding energy peaks of CTC@MOG@COP (CTC-Cr(Ⅵ)@MOG@COP) were divided into 185.08 (184.90) eV and 182.62 (182.55) eV, which were lower than 185.12 and 182.65 eV in the Zr 3d spectra of MOG@COP, illustrating the existence of coordination interaction (CD) between CTC molecules and uncoordinated metal sites (Fig. S19e) [40].

Furthermore, as noticed in Fig. S19f, the peaks of Cr 2p1/2 and Cr 2p3/2 clearly appeared at 586.56 (586.47) eV and 576.90 (576.47) eV in the Cr(Ⅵ)@MOG@COP and CTC-Cr(Ⅵ)@MOG@COP, indicating that a large amount of Cr(VI) can be efficiently captured in both single and binary systems [57]. Next, it is speculated that MOG@COP with − N= and CTC with − NH2 functional groups can produce the complexation effect (CP) with Cr(VI) ions to form complexes, which precisely explained the accelerated adsorption rate and increased q m of CTC in binary system [54,58]. On top of that, the C−C/C=C and O−C=O peaks of MOG@COP displaced to 284.62 (288.50) eV and 288.48 (288.47) eV in the Cr(Ⅵ)@MOG@COP and CTC-Cr(Ⅵ)@MOG@COP through anion-π interaction, proclaiming that anion-π interaction (A–π) was attendant [58]. Overall, in binary system, we speculate that might the steric hindrance of the shell COP in adsorbing Cr(Ⅵ) was enhanced by the speedy adsorption of CTC by Zr-MOG as the core of MOG@COP, simultaneously the CP between CTC and Cr(VI) was much less than the influence of steric hindrance on Cr(VI), causing the diminishment of in the q m of MOG@COP for Cr(Ⅵ) [58]. In conclusion, the reasonable adsorption mechanism of MOG@COP for CTC and Cr(Ⅵ) may be mainly determined by PF, ES, HB, HH, π–π EDA, CD, CP and A–π.

Notably, benefiting from the fluorescent COP component, the 2D and 3D excitation-emission spectra of MOG@COP were presented in Fig. 5c and Fig. S20. Considering the luminescence properties, whether MOG@COP can be used as a fluorescence probe for the rapid and quantitative determination of Cr(Ⅵ) in water environment was deeply discussed. First of all, time-oriented fluorescence intensities and corresponding quenching efficiencies were explored. Obviously, we can observe that the quenching efficiencies of MOG@COP reaching 62.08 % and 78.40 % at 1 min and 60 min, respectively (Fig. 4 a, b). Concretely, the adsorption process can bring the Cr(Ⅵ) in contact with the MOG@COP, which greatly improved the sensing efficiency of the MOG@COP and manifested as the synergistic effect of adsorption-detection. Concurrently, MOG@COP exhibited concentration-dependent fluorescence response toward Cr(Ⅵ) and the fluorescence intensity was constantly weakened with increasing Cr(Ⅵ) concentration in the range of 0–0.77 mM (Fig. 4c). On count of the sharp intensity decline under the concentration of Cr(Ⅵ) was 0.08 mM, it was naturally to conclude that MOG@COP was sensitive to low concentration of Cr(Ⅵ). Thereafter, lower concentrations of Cr(Ⅵ) in the range of 0 to 3.10 μM were selected to run the Stern-Volmer (SV) diagram (Eq. (S4)), obtaining the K SV value of 1.46*105 M−1 (Fig. 4d and Fig. 6a). The detection limit (LOD) (Eq. (S5)) calculated from the K SV value and standard deviation (SD) was 10 μg·L−1 (≈0.20 μM), which was lower than the maximum allowable limit of 50 μg·L−1 (≈1.00 μM) according to the WHO. In contrast with the majority of Cr(Ⅵ) detection sensors reported (Table S15), MOG@COP represented superior K SV value and lower LOD value. Distinctly, the MOG@COP fluorescent probe was competent enough to detect Cr(VI) in drinking water.

Apart from that, actual aqueous environment such as pH, ionic species and ionic strength may affect the fluorescence of MOG@COP. When various inorganic ions were interpolated into Cr(Ⅵ) solution, the influence of ionic species and strength on the fluorescence tied well with the adsorption of Cr(Ⅵ) by MOG@COP, meaning that there was a close relationship between the adsorption behavior of MOG@COP and the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4e ∼ h). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity in the range of pH=2.00 − 4.00 was higher than that under neutral and alkaline conditions (Fig. 4i), suggesting that it may be due to intermolecular/intramolecular hydrogen bondings under acidic conditions that caused MOG@COP to aggregate tightly [59,60].

To shed light on the fluorescence mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of MOG@COP at excitation wavelength of 372 nm was measured by the decay fluorescence lifetime measuring instrument. Based on nonlinear least squares analysis, the fluorescence attenuation curve could be well-fitted by a double-exponential function for the emission at 573 nm with CHISQ value of 1.305, implying that MOG@COP hold different and uniformly distributed emission sites [61]. The average fluorescence lifetime of Cr(VI) in the absence and presence was basically consistent, with values of 0.90 ns and 0.80 ns, respectively (Fig. 5 b), demonstrating that the fluorescence lifetime of MOG@COP was not extremely sensitive to the presence of Cr(VI). Thus, it can be proved that the whole process was a static quenching [62]. Since the resonance energy transfer (RET) mechanism caused a significant reduction in donor fluorescence lifetime during quenching, it can be speculated that RET may not work in our system. Besides, another evidence was using Stern-Volmer (SV) diagram to theoretically describe both static and dynamic quenching (Eq. (S6)) [63], wherein the quenching rate (K q) (1.62 × 1014 L∙(mol∙s−1)) was much higher than the maximum impact quenching constant (2.00 × 1010 L∙(mol∙s−1) [64]), once again testifying the presence of a static quenching process [65].

To delve deeper into the quenching mechanism, the significance of overlap integral between the UV–Vis absorption spectra of Cr(VI) and the fluorescence spectra of MOG@COP was enclosed. The results in Fig. 5c showed that Cr(VI) exhibited absorption peaks at 260 and 353 nm, respectively. When MOG@COP was excited at 372 nm, the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) basically coincided with the excitation spectrum of MOG@COP, making for the excitation light of MOG@COP being absorbed and shielded by Cr(VI) [66]. Therefore, fluorescence quenching based on the inner filter effect (IFE) may emerge (Fig. 5d). Overall, from these results it was clear that MOG@COP can be applied to actual condition with wide detection range and low detection limit of Cr(VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-based Cr(Ⅵ) sensing and anti-counterfeiting of MOG@COP/SA beads
ce:section.ce:para: For better adapting the detection in on-site application, shaped MOG@COP/SA beads with gradually darkened fluorescence associating with the increased Cr(Ⅵ) concentration were combined with the self-developed device and the smartphone-based colorimeter app under the UV irradiation of 365 nm (Fig. 6 c, d). To be precise, a good correlation can be established between the red (R) channel of MOG@COP/SA beads image and the concentration of Cr(Ⅵ) in binary system, with the standardized recoveries of 98.15 %−107.41 % and the relative standard deviations (RSD) < 3.14 % (Table S16). Beyond that, the K SV value of the MOG@COP/SA beads was 1.49*102 M−1 and the corresponding LOD value was 0.63 mM (Fig. 6b). This opened up a new way to realize instant visual fluorescence determination of detecting Cr(Ⅵ) on site.

Anti-counterfeiting technology was used to distinguish the authenticity and prevent counterfeiting, whose standard method was to encode information with a basic 1D bar code and an extended 2D QR code. In view of the fluorescence properties of MOG@COP/SA beads, they could be used as a new anti-counterfeiting candidate to improve the safety factor. The designed bar code and QR code made of MOG@COP/SA beads with “SLEL” information in Fig. 6e, f can be decrypted by smartphone under 365 nm UV irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of MOG@COP/SA beads in the adsorption-detection-anti-counterfeiting platform
ce:section.ce:para: The main design of the self-constructed adsorption-detection-anti-counterfeiting (ADA) platform was that it can flexibly select various functions such as “adsorption (A)”, “adsorption + detection (AD)”, “adsorption + detection + anti-counterfeiting (ADA)” in the light of actual demand. For the sake of sketching the practical application for undisturbed CTC and Cr(VI) capture, we envisioned shaped MOG@COP/SA beads with excellent adsorption behaviors into the A platform to treat with CTC-Cr(VI) co-contamination solutions under the condition of 1.00 mL·min−1 flow rate and 5.00 mg·L−1 concentration (Fig. 7 a). With GB 8978–1996 as the limit (0.50 mg·L−1), the purification volumes of Cr(VI) of 583.00 mL can reach the specified emission standard, and the removal rate of CTC can reach 100.00 % (Fig. 7b). More importantly, the fluorescent image of MOG@COP/SA beads in the AD platform can be detected and further converted to the red (R) channel value to intuitively reflect the residual amount of Cr(VI) in water (Fig. 7c). Ultimately, the pre-designed QR code based on MOG@COP/SA beads could ensure the anti-counterfeiting of the ADA platform. In a word, this signified that MOG@COP/SA beads, as a kind of multi-functional adsorbent-detector-anti-counterfeiting agent, can provide attractive feedback for practical applications of co-adsorption, mono-detection and anti-counterfeiting in CTC-Cr(VI) co-contamination dynamic system.

In summary, a novel porous and luminescent core–shell hybrid material (MOG@COP) was properly prepared by encapsulating Zr-MOG into COP, followed by embedding MOG@COP into SA matrix to obtain shaped MOG@COP/SA beads with easy separation. Then, the adsorptive decontamination processes and adsorption mechanism of MOG@COP and MOG@COP/SA beads on CTC and Cr(Ⅵ) in both single and binary systems were analyzed. Simultaneously, fluorescence endowed laboratory sensing and real-time detection were applied to estimate the concentration of Cr(Ⅵ) in a sequence. What’s more, the MOG@COP/SA beads were extended to the self-constructed ADA platform, which realized the joint capture of CTC and Cr(Ⅵ), the naked eye visual monitoring of Cr(Ⅵ) residual concentration as well as the optical anti-counterfeiting protection, and became a multi-functional continuous adsorptive remover, visual detector and authenticity-ensure anti-counterfeiting agent. All in all, one can envisage that this study has achieved the following goals: (i) furnishing reference information for constructing recycled multi-functional MOG@COP/SA beads; (ii) providing valuable insights into the combined removal-detection in the co-contamination system of antibiotics and heavy metals for wastewater issues; (iii) laying a foundation for the further development of anti-counterfeiting applications.
